records.ety: [L. prospectus, fr. prospicere, prospectum, to look forward; pro before, forward + specere, spicere, look, to see: cf. OF. prospect. See Spy, v., and cf. Prospectus.]

defs: That which is embraced by eye in vision; the region which the eye overlooks at one time; view; scene; outlook.
quotes: His eye discovers unaware The goodly prospect of some foreign land.

defs: Especially, a picturesque or widely extended view; a landscape; hence, a sketch of a landscape.
quotes: I went to Putney . . . to take prospects in crayon.

defs: A position affording a fine view; a lookout.
quotes: Him God beholding from his prospect high.

defs: Relative position of the front of a building or other structure; face; relative aspect.
quotes: And their prospect was toward the south.

defs: The act of looking forward; foresight; anticipation; as, a prospect of the future state.
quotes: Is he a prudent man as to his temporal estate, that lays designs only for a day, without any prospect to, or provision for, the remaining part of life ?

defs: That which is hoped for; ground for hope or expectation; expectation; probable result; as, the prospect of success.
quotes: These swell their prospectsd exalt their pride, When offers are disdain'd, and love deny'd.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Prospected; p. pr. & vb. n. Prospecting.]

defs: To look over; to explore or examine for something; as, to prospect a district for gold.
To make a search; to seek; to explore, as for mines or the like; as, to prospect for gold.
